doctor.name: Dr. Atmaranjan Dash
doctor.title: MBBS | MS | MCh
doctor.tagline:
Chief Consultant, Neurosurgery,
CARE Hospital, Bhubaneswar
doctor.summary: Dr. Atmaranjan Dash is a distinguished and pioneering neurosurgeon based in Bhubaneswar, India. Renowned as the first neurosurgeon in India to perform unilateral bi-portal endoscopy, he has consistently pushed the boundaries of medical innovation. With extensive expertise in endoscopic spine surgery, Dr. Dash has significantly contributed to the field of neurosurgery through his clinical practice, teaching, and research. He is an accomplished academician with numerous national and international publications and a respected faculty member in premier neurosurgical societies.
qualifications: MBBS from MKCG Medical College, Berhampur
MS - General Surgery from SCB Medical College, Cuttack
MCh - Neurosurgery from AIIMS, New Delhi
MRCS from the Royal College of Surgeons, Edinburgh
certifications: Fellowship in Minimally Invasive Spine Surgery - National University Hospital, Singapore
Fellowship in Skull Base Surgery - University of Pittsburgh Medical Center, USA
Fellowship in Cerebrovascular Surgery - Tokyo Women's Medical University, Japan
Senior Fellowship in Complex Spine Surgery - London, UK

highlights.text: Spearheads the neurosurgery department with a focus on minimally invasive and endoscopic procedures.
Director of the International UBE Training Program, mentoring surgeons in advanced techniques.
Innovator of India’s first augmented reality endoscopic spine surgery using Apple Vision Pro.
Pioneered the first unilateral bi-portal endoscopy (UBE) in India, revolutionizing spine surgery.
Extensive experience in complex brain tumor resections, cerebrovascular surgeries, and spinal deformity corrections.
Renowned for expertise in revision spine surgeries and managing failed back syndrome cases.
Active contributor to neurosurgical research with numerous publications in top-tier journals.
Regularly invited as a speaker and faculty at national and international neurosurgery conferences.

memberships: ISUBI (International Society of Unilateral Biportal Endoscopy)
President, Radical Association of Spine Endoscopic Society
academicContributions: Prime Faculty Member in Endoscopic Society of India, NSI, and ESFI.
Conducts workshops and training sessions on endoscopic and minimally invasive spine surgery.
Published extensively in leading national and international journals.

testimonials.name: Hemant Kumar
testimonials.review: Sir did my spinal surgery three years ago. Now I am completely fine and can do everything like a normal person in daily life.

testimonials.name: Ramesh Patra
testimonials.review: Very good behaviour of doctor to the patient and attendant. Good advice and sincere working. Before operation I could not stand straight but now I am standing easily and perfectly.

faqs.question: When should I see a neurosurgeon?
faqs.answer: You should consult a neurosurgeon if you experience severe or persistent headaches, chronic back or neck pain, numbness or tingling in arms or legs, sudden weakness, loss of balance, seizures, memory problems, or head and spine injury. Early consultation can prevent serious complications.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after brain surgery?
faqs.answer: Recovery depends on the type and complexity of surgery. Hospital stay may be 3-7 days, initial recovery 2-4 weeks, full recovery 6-12 weeks, and return to work 4-8 weeks depending on job type. Dr. Dash provides personalized recovery plans and follow-up care.

faqs.question: What conditions does Dr. Dash treat?
faqs.answer: Dr. Dash specializes in brain tumors, spine disorders, stroke and cerebrovascular diseases, head and spine trauma, nerve compression, and pediatric neurosurgery.

faqs.question: Does insurance cover neurosurgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, most major insurance providers cover neurosurgical procedures. CARE Hospital is empaneled with CGHS, state government insurance schemes, private health insurance companies, and Ayushman Bharat PMJAY.

faqs.question: What is minimally invasive spine surgery?
faqs.answer: Minimally invasive spine surgery uses smaller incisions, often 1-2 cm, compared to traditional open surgery. Benefits include less muscle damage, reduced blood loss, faster recovery, shorter hospital stay, and less post-operative pain.

faqs.question: Is spine surgery always necessary for back pain?
faqs.answer: No. Surgery is recommended only when conservative treatments fail, there is progressive nerve damage or weakness, severe pain affects daily life, or there is loss of bladder or bowel control. Dr. Dash explores non-surgical options first.

faqs.question: How can I book an appointment?
faqs.answer: You can book an appointment through WhatsApp, call 9439971946, or visit CARE Hospital, Bhubaneswar. Same-day appointments are available for emergency cases.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a stroke?
faqs.answer: Remember FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, and Time to call for immediate medical attention. Early treatment saves lives.

blogPosts.title: Neurological Deficit: When Immediate Spine Surgery Becomes Necessary
blogPosts.excerpt: Progressive weakness, foot drop, urinary retention, and spinal cord compression may require urgent surgical treatment to prevent permanent neurological damage.
blogPosts.author: Dr. Atmaranjan Dash
blogPosts.content:
Emergency Spine Care

Neurological Deficits:When Spine Surgery Becomes an Emergency

Neurological deficits are among the most critical indications for urgent spine surgery. When nerves or the spinal cord become compressed, delayed treatment can result in permanent weakness, loss of function, or irreversible nerve damage.

Warning Signs You Should Never Ignore

Certain symptoms indicate that the spinal cord or nerve roots may be under significant pressure and require immediate medical attention.

Progressive Weakness

Weakness in arms or legs that worsens over time.

Foot Drop

Difficulty lifting the front part of the foot.

Quadriparesis

Weakness affecting all four limbs.

Paraparesis

Weakness affecting both lower limbs.

Urinary Retention

Loss of bladder control or difficulty urinating.

Saddle Anesthesia

Numbness around the groin and inner thighs.

Seek Immediate Medical Attention

If any of these symptoms develop or worsen, urgent evaluation by a spine specialist is strongly recommended.

Conditions That Often Require Urgent Surgery

Cervical Myelopathy

Compression of the spinal cord in the neck region can result in balance problems, hand clumsiness, weakness, and progressive disability.

Massive Lumbar Disc Herniation

Large disc prolapse causing severe nerve compression and potential cauda equina syndrome.

Spinal Tumors

Tumors may compress the spinal cord or nerves and cause progressive neurological deficits.

Epidural Hematoma

Bleeding around the spinal cord requiring urgent decompression.

Epidural Abscess

Spinal infection causing pus collection and risk of paralysis.

How Quickly Should Surgery Be Performed?

Depending on the underlying cause and severity of neurological impairment, surgery may be required immediately or within 24–48 hours to maximize neurological recovery and prevent permanent disability.

Key Takeaway

Neurological deficits should never be ignored. Progressive weakness, bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or difficulty walking may indicate serious spinal cord or nerve compression. Early diagnosis and timely surgery can significantly improve outcomes and prevent permanent neurological damage.

blogPosts.title: Persistent Spine Pain Despite Treatment: When Surgery May Help
blogPosts.excerpt: Discover when severe neck or back pain that does not respond to medications, physiotherapy, or injections may require surgical intervention.
blogPosts.author: Dr. Atmaranjan Dash
blogPosts.content:
Modern Spine Care

Finding Relief: When Is It Time to Consider Spine Surgery?

Most people dealing with back or neck pain improve with conservative treatments such as physical therapy, medications, lifestyle modifications, and rest. However, when symptoms persist despite these measures, spine surgery may become an important option for long-term relief and improved quality of life.

When Is Spine Surgery Considered?

Spine surgery is rarely the first treatment option. Specialists generally recommend surgery only when specific clinical criteria are met and conservative treatment has failed to provide adequate relief.

Duration of Symptoms

Persistent symptoms lasting 6–12 weeks or longer without meaningful improvement.

Severe Nerve Pain

Intense shooting pain radiating down the arms or legs due to nerve compression.

Lifestyle Limitation

Difficulty performing daily activities, working, exercising, or sleeping comfortably.

Confirmed Diagnosis

MRI or advanced imaging reveals structural abnormalities matching clinical symptoms.

Surgery Is Not Always the First Choice

Most spine specialists recommend exhausting non-surgical treatments before considering surgery unless there is a neurological emergency or severe spinal instability.

Common Conditions That May Require Surgery

In many cases, surgery becomes necessary because structural problems continue to place pressure on spinal nerves despite conservative care.

Lumbar Disc Herniation

A spinal disc bulges or ruptures and compresses nearby nerve roots, leading to sciatica, weakness, and persistent pain.

Foraminal Stenosis

Narrowing of the nerve exit channels causes ongoing nerve irritation, numbness, weakness, and radiating pain.

Lateral Recess Stenosis

Compression occurs where nerves travel before exiting the spinal canal, resulting in chronic pain and neurological symptoms.

The Benefits of Modern Spine Surgery

Today's spine surgery techniques are significantly more advanced than traditional open procedures. Many patients can now benefit from minimally invasive and endoscopic approaches that reduce tissue damage and accelerate recovery.

Targeted Relief

Precise decompression of affected nerves with minimal disruption.

Smaller Incisions

Reduced muscle injury, less blood loss, and lower postoperative discomfort.

Faster Recovery

Earlier mobility, shorter hospital stays, and quicker return to normal activities.

Key Takeaway

If you have been struggling with persistent back or neck pain for several weeks or months despite appropriate treatment, it may be time to consult a spine specialist. Modern minimally invasive and endoscopic spine procedures can provide effective relief while helping patients recover faster and return to their daily lives with confidence.

blogPosts.title: Understanding Spinal Instability and When Fusion Surgery Is Needed
blogPosts.excerpt: Spinal instability can lead to pain, deformity, and neurological symptoms. Learn when surgical stabilization becomes necessary.
blogPosts.author: Dr. Atmaranjan Dash
blogPosts.content:
Introduction

The spine must maintain proper alignment and stability during movement. When stability is lost, surgery may be required.

Causes of Spinal Instability

  • Spondylolisthesis
  • Traumatic fractures
  • Degenerative deformities
  • Post-laminectomy instability

Treatment Options

  • Spinal fusion surgery
  • Instrumentation using rods and screws

Goals of Surgery

The primary goals are to restore stability, reduce pain, and prevent further neurological deterioration.

blogPosts.title: Spinal Cord Compression and Myelopathy: Why Early Surgery Matters
blogPosts.excerpt: Cervical and thoracic myelopathy can cause progressive disability. Early diagnosis and surgical treatment often provide the best outcomes.
blogPosts.author: Dr. Atmaranjan Dash
blogPosts.content:
Introduction

Spinal cord compression can lead to progressive neurological dysfunction if not treated promptly.

Symptoms of Myelopathy

  • Hand clumsiness
  • Difficulty walking
  • Balance problems
  • Hyperreflexia
  • Positive Hoffmann sign
  • Positive Babinski sign

Common Causes

  • Cervical canal stenosis
  • OPLL
  • Thoracic disc herniation

Importance of Early Surgery

Timely decompression helps prevent irreversible spinal cord damage and improves neurological recovery.

blogPosts.title: Spinal Tumors, Infections, and Structural Lesions Requiring Surgery
blogPosts.excerpt: Certain spinal conditions almost always require surgery due to compression of nerves or the spinal cord.
blogPosts.author: Dr. Atmaranjan Dash
blogPosts.content:
Introduction

Some structural spinal abnormalities require surgical removal because they can threaten neurological function and spinal stability.

Conditions Often Requiring Surgery

  • Intradural tumors
  • Extradural tumors
  • Spinal infections with compression
  • Severe spinal deformities
  • Recurrent disc prolapse

Why Surgery Is Recommended

These conditions may compress nerves, the spinal cord, or destabilize the spine, making surgical intervention the most effective treatment.

Expected Outcomes

Early diagnosis and treatment can improve outcomes and help preserve neurological function.
